fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need to ship a token utility tool that our backend services can use for auth flows. The basic idea is: given some kind of signing key and a blob of user data, produce a tamper-proof string that other services can validate later. We also need the reverse — take one of those strings, check it's legit, and hand back the original data. Should support all the usual key flavors our infra team uses (shared secrets, RSA certs, elliptic curve keys, and that newer Edwards curve stuff). Oh and we talked about this in the last sprint — remember how we handled the 'time window' logic in the session module? Do the same thing here, tokens should be rejectable if they're stale or not yet valid. Keys also need to travel over the wire, so there's that JSON key format everyone uses — export and import should both work. The tool reads a single JSON blob from stdin and writes a result to stdout, similar to our other CLI utilities. Non-technical folks on ops need readable error labels, not stack traces — so normalize all failures into categories. One last thing: payloads can be either a plain string or a structured object, but if someone passes something weird like a raw number we should call that out explicitly. Reach out if the key format specs are unclear, I can dig up the exact field names from the standards doc.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: void main() async { final input = jsonDecode(await stdin.transform(utf8.decoder).join()); final result = await dispatch(input); stdout.write(result); }

trigger_keywords: how should the token header look
what fields go in the JWT header
header format for signed tokens
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JWT header must always contain exactly two fields: 'alg' set to the algorithm identifier (e.g. HS256, RS256, ES256, EdDSA) and 'typ' set to the string 'JWT'. These must be base64url-encoded without padding as the first segment of the compact serialization.

trigger_keywords: should iat be added automatically
issued-at claim behavior
when is the iat field stamped
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The signing operation automatically injects an 'iat' (issued-at) claim with the current Unix timestamp into map payloads unless the 'no_iat' flag is set to true in the command. String payloads are not modified. The 'no_iat' flag is used in tests to make output deterministic.

trigger_keywords: how is expiry enforced
what happens with expired tokens
exp claim checking behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During verification, if the payload contains an 'exp' claim and the current time is past that timestamp, the adapter must output exactly 'error=token_expired\n' (single line, no reason line). This check can be disabled by passing 'check_exp: false' in the verify command.

trigger_keywords: not-before claim enforcement
nbf rejection behavior
token not yet active error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: During verification, if the payload contains an 'nbf' claim and the current time is before that timestamp, the adapter must output exactly 'error=token_not_active\n' (single line, no reason line). This check can be disabled by passing 'check_nbf: false' in the verify command.

trigger_keywords: issue_at threshold check
minimum issued-at validation
iat lower bound enforcement
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The verify command accepts an optional 'issue_at' integer field. If provided, the token's 'iat' claim must be greater than or equal to this threshold value. If the token's iat is strictly less than the provided issue_at value, output 'error=invalid_token\nreason=expired_issue_at\n'. If no 'issue_at' is provided, future iat values are accepted without error.

trigger_keywords: what payload types are accepted
can payload be a number
invalid payload type error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only JSON string and JSON object (map) payloads are accepted for signing. If the payload field is a JSON number, boolean, array, or null, the adapter must output exactly 'error=invalid_payload_type\n'. A string payload is base64url-encoded as raw UTF-8 bytes without any JSON wrapping. A map payload is serialized to canonical JSON (keys sorted) before encoding.

trigger_keywords: verification output format
what does a successful verify print
alg and payload output on verify
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A successful verification must emit exactly two lines: first 'alg=<ALGORITHM>' where the algorithm name is taken from the verified token header, then 'payload=<canonical-json>' where the JSON object has its keys sorted lexicographically. The entire output ends with a trailing newline.

trigger_keywords: invalid signature error format
bad token structure output
invalid_token reason slug
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When a token fails signature verification or cannot be parsed (malformed base64url, wrong number of segments, unparseable header/payload JSON), the adapter must output 'error=invalid_token
reason=invalid_signature
' (two lines). Other structural parse failures output 'error=parse_error
reason=<slug>
'.

trigger_keywords: JWK export format
to_jwk canonical output
how are keys serialized to JWK
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'to_jwk' operation outputs a single line of canonical JSON with keys sorted. For 'oct' (secret) keys: fields are 'k' (base64url of secret bytes), 'kty': 'oct', 'use': 'sig'. An optional 'with_alg' field in the command adds an 'alg' field to the JWK. EC private keys always include 'alg', 'crv', 'd', 'kty', 'use', 'x', 'y'. EdDSA keys include 'alg': 'EdDSA', 'crv': 'Ed25519', 'kty': 'OKP', 'use': 'sig', and 'd'/'x' for private/public respectively.

trigger_keywords: JWK import output format
from_jwk response lines
what does importing a JWK print
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'from_jwk' operation outputs exactly two lines: 'kind=<category>' where category is one of secret, rsa_private, rsa_public, ec_private, ec_public, ed_private, ed_public; and 'kty=<key-type>' where key-type is one of oct, RSA, EC, OKP. The presence of a 'd' field in an asymmetric JWK indicates a private key.

trigger_keywords: ECDSA deterministic signing
ES256K secp256k1 support
which curves are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: ECDSA signing must use RFC 6979 deterministic nonce generation so signatures are reproducible. Four algorithm identifiers are supported: ES256 (P-256 curve, SHA-256), ES384 (P-384, SHA-384), ES512 (P-521, SHA-512), and ES256K (secp256k1, SHA-256). The signature is encoded as the raw R||S concatenation (not DER) in base64url, with each component zero-padded to the curve's coordinate byte length.

trigger_keywords: key kind field values
what are the valid key categories
key object structure in commands
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every key object in a command must have a 'kind' field. Valid values are: 'secret' (carries a 'secret' string field), 'rsa_private', 'rsa_public', 'ec_private', 'ec_public', 'ed_private', 'ed_public' (all carry a 'pem' string field containing the PEM-encoded key). No other fields are required. The kind determines which key abstraction is instantiated.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'sign', 'verify', 'to_jwk', 'from_jwk'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: remember how we handled the 'time window' logic in the session module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Claim validation for 'exp', 'nbf', and 'iat' fields as specified in feature3_1_exp.json, feature3_2_nbf.json, and feature3_3_issue_at.json — including the check_exp/check_nbf disable flags and the issue_at threshold parameter

context_pointers.vague_reference: that JSON key format everyone uses — export and import should both work
context_pointers.actual_meaning: JWK (JSON Web Key) serialization and deserialization via 'to_jwk' and 'from_jwk' operations as specified in feature5_1_export_jwk.json and feature5_2_import_jwk.json, including canonical JSON output with sorted keys

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to our other CLI utilities
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter reads exactly one JSON object from stdin, processes the 'op' field to dispatch to sign/verify/to_jwk/from_jwk operations, and writes plain-text line-oriented results to stdout with a trailing newline

Error output must use exactly these normalized category strings: 'error=token_expired', 'error=token_not_active', 'error=invalid_payload_type', 'error=invalid_token' (with a 'reason=' second line), or 'error=parse_error' (with a 'reason=' second line) — never raw exception messages or stack traces

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect in my notes right now. Could you check the existing session/auth module patterns in the codebase, or look at the standards doc for the JWT/JWK spec? If it's about error formatting or output structure, follow whatever the other CLI tools in the project are already doing.

feature1_1_sign_hmac.json.description: Issue a compact token from a map payload using an HMAC shared-secret algorithm; the output is the full compact serialization (header.payload.signature).

feature1_2_sign_rsa_pkcs1.json.description: Issue a compact token from a map payload using an RSA PKCS#1 v1.5 private key; deterministic signature.

feature1_3_sign_ecdsa.json.description: Issue a compact token from a map payload using an elliptic-curve private key (deterministic ECDSA) across the P-256, P-384, P-521 and secp256k1 curves.

key.pem:
-----BEGIN EC PRIVATE KEY-----
MHQCAQEEINCRiJnNDnzfo2So2tWY4AIuzeC2ZBp/hmMDcZz3Fh45oAcGBSuBBAAK
oUQDQgAE0aELkvG/Xeo5y6o0WXRAjlediLptGz7Q8zjDmpGFXkKBYZ6IiL7JJ2Tk
cHzd83bmeUeGX33RGTYFPXs5t/VBnw==
-----END EC PRIVATE KEY-----

feature1_4_sign_eddsa.json.description: Issue a compact token from a map payload using an Edwards-curve (Ed25519) private key.

feature2_1_verify_hmac.json.description: Validate an HMAC-signed compact token with the shared secret and return the embedded algorithm and claims.

feature2_2_verify_rsa_pkcs1.json.description: Validate an RSA PKCS#1 v1.5 signed compact token with the RSA public key and return algorithm and claims.

feature2_3_verify_ecdsa.json.description: Validate an ECDSA signed compact token with the matching public key across P-256, P-384, P-521 and secp256k1.

feature2_4_verify_rsa_pss.json.description: Validate an RSA-PSS signed compact token with the RSA public key and return algorithm and claims.

feature2_5_verify_eddsa.json.description: Validate an Ed25519 signed compact token with the Edwards-curve public key and return algorithm and claims.

feature3_1_exp.json.description: Enforce the expiration (exp) claim during verification: a token past its expiry is rejected, a token within validity returns its claims, and expiry checking can be switched off.

feature3_2_nbf.json.description: Enforce the not-before (nbf) claim during verification: a token not yet active is rejected, an already-active token returns its claims, and the not-before check can be switched off.

feature3_3_issue_at.json.description: Enforce an issued-at lower bound during verification: with a minimum issued-at threshold the token passes when its iat is at or after the threshold and is rejected when its iat predates it; with no threshold a future iat is accepted.

feature4_payload_types.json.description: Accept string and map payloads when issuing a token but reject a numeric payload as an invalid payload type.

feature5_1_export_jwk.json.description: Export a key to its JWK JSON object. Secret and RSA keys omit the algorithm hint unless requested; elliptic-curve and Edwards-curve keys always include their algorithm.

feature5_2_import_jwk.json.description: Import a JWK JSON object and report the resolved key category and key type for secret, RSA, elliptic-curve and Edwards-curve public and private keys.
